q: The SI unit of electric current is:
sol: Electric current is measured in Amperes (A), named after André-Marie Ampère.

q: One Coulomb is equivalent to the charge contained in nearly how many electrons?
The instrument used to measure electric current in a circuit is:
sol: An ammeter is always connected in series to measure the current flowing through a circuit.

q: The work done in moving a unit charge from one point to another is called:
sol: Potential difference (V) = Work done (W) / Charge (Q).

q: 1 Volt is equal to:
sol: V = W/Q, so 1 V = 1 J / 1 C.

q: Which device is used to maintain a potential difference across a conductor?
sol: A chemical cell or a battery provides the energy to maintain potential difference.

q: Ohm's law states the relationship between:
sol: V = IR; it relates potential difference (V) to current (I) for a conductor.

q: The SI unit of resistance is:
sol: Resistance is measured in Ohms (Ω).

q: If the potential difference across a resistor is doubled, the current will:
sol: According to V ∝ I, doubling V will double I if resistance is constant.

q: A variable resistor used to change the current in a circuit without changing the voltage source is:
sol: A rheostat allows the user to vary resistance, thereby controlling the current.

q: The resistance of a conductor is directly proportional to its:
sol: R ∝ l; longer conductors have more resistance.

q: When the area of cross-section of a wire is doubled, its resistance becomes:
sol: R ∝ 1/A; doubling the area halves the resistance.

q: The SI unit of resistivity is:
sol: Resistivity (ρ) = RA/l, which gives Ω * m^2 / m = Ωm.

q: Which of the following has the lowest resistivity?
sol: Silver is the best conductor and has the lowest resistivity among the options.

q: Alloys like constantan and manganin are used for making standard resistors because:
o: Their resistivity changes rapidly with temperature
They do not oxidize easily at high temperatures
sol: Alloys have high resistivity and high melting points/oxidation resistance.

q: When resistors are connected in series, the _____ remains the same through each resistor.
sol: In a series circuit, there is only one path for the current.

q: In a parallel circuit, the _____ across each resistor is the same.
sol: All components in parallel are connected to the same two nodes.

q: The equivalent resistance of three 3Ω resistors connected in series is:
The equivalent resistance of three 3Ω resistors connected in parallel is:
The rate at which electric energy is dissipated or consumed is called:
sol: Power is the rate of doing work or consuming energy (P = VI).

q: The SI unit of electric power is:
sol: Power is measured in Watts (W).

q: One kilowatt-hour (kWh) is equal to how many Joules?
Commercial unit of electrical energy is:
sol: kWh is the unit used by utility companies for billing.

q: Which formula represents Joule's law of heating?
sol: All three formulas are equivalent representations of the heat produced.

q: Tungsten is used for the filament of electric bulbs because:
o: It has a high melting point
sol: Tungsten's high melting point (3380°C) allows it to glow white-hot without melting.

q: Electric bulbs are usually filled with chemically inactive gases like:
sol: These gases prevent the oxidation of the filament and prolong its life.

q: A fuse wire is always connected in _____ with the live wire.
sol: A fuse must be in series to break the circuit if current exceeds the limit.

q: The material used for making a fuse wire should have:
sol: It must melt easily when excess current flows to protect the circuit.

q: An electric iron consumes 1 kW power when operated at 220 V. The current is:
If the length of a wire is doubled and its radius is halved, the new resistance is:
sol: R ∝ l/r². New R ∝ (2l)/(r/2)² = 8(l/r²). Resistance becomes 8 times.

q: How much work is done in moving a charge of 2 C across two points having a potential difference of 12 V?
Which of the following does not represent electrical power?
sol: P = VI = I²R = V²/R. IR² is not a valid formula for power.

q: Ammeters are designed to have very ____ resistance.
sol: Low resistance ensures it doesn't change the current it is meant to measure.

q: Voltmeters are connected in ____ to the component being measured.
sol: Voltmeters measure potential difference across two points, so they must be in parallel.

q: When 'n' resistors each of resistance 'R' are connected in parallel, the effective resistance is:
sol: 1/Rp = 1/R + 1/R... (n times) = n/R, so Rp = R/n.

q: Electric charge flows through a conductor when there is a difference of:
sol: Electric pressure (potential difference) is required for charge flow.

q: The direction of conventional electric current is taken as:
sol: Historically, current is defined as the flow of positive charge (opposite to electrons).

q: Which physical quantity has the unit Volt/Ampere?
sol: By Ohm's law, R = V/I, so unit is V/A or Ohm.

q: Resistance of a metallic conductor ____ with increase in temperature.
sol: Increased temperature causes more collisions of electrons with metal ions.

q: If 0.5 A current flows through a wire for 10 minutes, the amount of charge is:
A piece of wire of resistance R is cut into five equal parts. These parts are then connected in parallel. If the equivalent resistance of this combination is R', then the ratio R/R' is:
sol: Each part is R/5. In parallel: 1/R' = 5/(R/5) + 5/(R/5)... (5 times) = 25/R. R/R' = 25.

q: An electric lamp of 100 Ω and a toaster of 50 Ω are connected in parallel to a 220 V source. What is the total current?
Electrical resistivity of a given metallic wire depends upon:
sol: Resistivity is a characteristic property of the material, not dimensions.

q: Maximum resistance can be obtained by connecting resistors in:
sol: Series connection adds individual resistances: Rs = R1 + R2...

q: Minimum resistance can be obtained by connecting resistors in:
sol: Parallel connection results in an equivalent resistance smaller than the smallest component.

q: Two conducting wires of the same material and of equal lengths and equal diameters are first connected in series and then parallel in a circuit across the same potential difference. The ratio of heat produced in series and parallel combinations would be:
sol: Rs = 2R, Rp = R/2. H = V²t/R. Hs/Hp = (V²/2R) / (V²/(R/2)) = 1/4.

q: How many 176 Ω resistors (in parallel) are required to carry 5 A on a 220 V line?
What is the maximum resistance which can be made using five resistors each of 1/5 Ω?
sol: Max resistance is in series: 1/5 + 1/5 + 1/5 + 1/5 + 1/5 = 1 Ω.

q: The resistivity of insulators is in the range of:
sol: Insulators have very high resistivity to prevent flow of current.

q: Which of the following graphs represents Ohm's Law correctly?
sol: V is directly proportional to I, so the V-I graph is a linear straight line through the origin.

q: If R1 and R2 are two resistors in parallel, the total resistance R is:
sol: 1/R = 1/R1 + 1/R2 = (R1+R2)/R1R2. Inverting gives R1R2/(R1+R2).

q: The power of an appliance is 100 W. If it is used for 10 hours, energy consumed is:
sol: Energy = Power * Time = 100W * 10h = 1000 Wh = 1 kWh.

q: When a 4Ω resistor is connected across a 12V battery, the current is:
Joule's heating effect is used in:
sol: Toasters convert electrical energy into heat energy.

q: If the current through a resistor is halved, the heat produced becomes:
sol: H ∝ I². If I becomes I/2, H becomes (I/2)² = 1/4.

q: The unit of electric potential is named after:
sol: The Volt is named in honor of the Italian physicist Alessandro Volta.

q: The reciprocal of resistance is called:
sol: Conductance (G) = 1/R.

q: A multimeter can measure:
sol: A multimeter is a versatile instrument for measuring multiple electrical properties.

q: Why is copper used for electrical transmission lines?
sol: Low resistivity reduces energy loss during transmission.

q: The slope of V-I graph (V on y-axis, I on x-axis) represents:
sol: Slope = ΔV/ΔI = R.

q: What happens to the current in a circuit if resistance is doubled and voltage remains same?
sol: I = V/R. If R becomes 2R, I becomes I/2.

q: In domestic circuits, the appliances are connected in:
sol: Parallel connection allows each appliance to operate at the same voltage and be switched independently.

q: Which of the following is a semiconductor?
sol: Silicon has properties between conductors and insulators.

q: The charge on an electron is:
sol: Electrons carry a negative charge of 1.6 x 10^-19 Coulombs.

q: A cell converts _____ energy into electrical energy.
sol: Chemical reactions inside the cell generate a potential difference.

q: If 'V' is the potential difference and 'I' is the current, electric power 'P' is:
sol: P = VI is the fundamental definition of electric power.

q: The heating element of an electric heater is made of:
sol: Nichrome (alloy) has high resistivity and high melting point.

q: Safety wire 'fuse' works on the principle of:
sol: The wire melts due to the heat generated by excess current.

q: The electrical energy consumed by a 50W bulb in 2 hours is:
sol: Energy = 50W * 2h = 100 Wh = 0.1 kWh.

q: Resistance of a conductor does not depend on:
sol: Resistance is a physical property, though current depends on V, R itself is constant for an ohmic conductor.

q: One Ampere is defined as:
sol: I = Q/t, so 1 A = 1 Coulomb / 1 second.

q: If two identical resistors are connected in series, the equivalent resistance is 20 Ω. Their individual resistance is:
If the same two 10 Ω resistors are connected in parallel, the resistance is:
Which of the following is a non-ohmic conductor?
sol: Semiconductors do not follow a linear V-I relationship.

q: The potential difference is measured using a:
sol: Voltmeters measure electric potential difference between two points.

q: Electric current is a _____ quantity.
sol: Even though it has direction, it follows scalar addition laws.

q: When light bulbs are connected in series, if one bulb blows out:
sol: Series connection breaks the entire path if one component fails.

q: In a parallel connection of lamps, if one lamp fails:
sol: Parallel paths are independent.

q: An electric oven rated 2 kW is used in a household having a 220 V supply. The fuse rating should be:
sol: I = P/V = 2000/220 ≈ 9.09 A. A 10 A fuse is appropriate.

q: The potential of the earth is taken as:
sol: Earth is the reference point for electric potential.

q: Which of the following is used as a safety measure in electrical circuits?
sol: Fuses protect against short circuits and overloading.

q: The resistivity of alloys is generally _____ than that of their constituent metals.
sol: Alloys are engineered to have higher resistance.

q: Electric power is inversely proportional to _____ when voltage is constant.
sol: P = V²/R. If V is constant, P ∝ 1/R.

q: A wire of resistance R is stretched to double its length. New resistance is:
sol: Volume remains same. If l doubles, A halves. R' = ρ(2l)/(A/2) = 4R.

q: Which of the following has high resistance?
sol: Manganin (alloy) has much higher resistance than pure metals.

q: The unit of resistivity 'Ohm-meter' can be written as:
sol: Ωm is the standard unit for resistivity.

q: In the formula V = IR, 'I' stands for:
sol: I is the standard symbol for electric current.

q: If the diameter of a wire is halved, its resistance becomes:
sol: R ∝ 1/d². If d becomes d/2, R becomes 4R.

q: How much energy is given to each coulomb of charge passing through a 6 V battery?
sol: Energy = V * Q = 6V * 1C = 6 J.

q: The current flowing through a conductor is 2 A when the PD is 10 V. The resistance is:
Two resistors of 2 Ω and 4 Ω are connected in series to a 6 V battery. The current in the circuit is:
Power of a bulb is 60 W at 220 V. Its resistance is:
A thick wire has _____ resistance than a thin wire of same material and length.
sol: Thicker wires have a larger area of cross-section, reducing resistance.

q: A dry cell produces a potential difference of about:
sol: Most standard AA/AAA dry cells are rated at 1.5 V.

q: The work done in moving a charge Q through a potential difference V is:
sol: By definition of potential difference, W = VQ.

q: Which of the following is not a unit of energy?
sol: Watt is a unit of power, not energy.

q: Electrical conductivity is the reciprocal of:
sol: Conductivity (σ) = 1/ρ.

q: When two or more resistors are connected in parallel, the total resistance is _____ the smallest resistor.
sol: Adding parallel paths always reduces the total resistance below any individual branch.

q: The symbol for Ohm is:
sol: Ω (Omega) is the Greek letter used for Ohms.

q: The heat produced in a wire is ____ to the square of current.
sol: H = I²Rt shows the square dependence on current.
